results.possibleIdentity: Peter Ballantine (often spelled Balentine in informal contexts) is an artist, sculptor, and master woodworker best known for his significant contributions to the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) projects and his extensive collaboration with minimalist artist Donald Judd. Active in the New York art scene since the late 1960s, Ballantine was a fellow at the Whitney Museum Independent Study Program in 1968. He is historically linked to E.A.T. through his work on the 'New York Collection for Stockholm' (1971–1973), for which he designed and hand-built the Honduran mahogany boxes used to house a major portfolio of 30 prints by iconic artists such as Robert Rauschenberg, Andy Warhol, and Richard Serra. Beginning in 1969, he became a primary fabricator for Donald Judd, eventually constructing nearly 250 of Judd’s plywood works and supervising others. Today, he is considered a leading authority on Judd’s philosophy, having curated over 40 exhibitions and lectured extensively on the relationship between authorship and fabrication in contemporary art.

Peter Balentine is a contemporary documentary and fine-art photographer and an ordained pastor based in Lynn, Massachusetts. He holds a bachelor’s degree in English Literature from Boston University and advanced degrees in theology from Gordon-Conwell Theological Seminary. His artistic work focuses on bridging the worlds of faith and art, most notably through his multi-year documentary project 'City of God,' which explores the lives of immigrant faith communities. His photography has been exhibited at the Griffin Museum of Photography, the Galleries of Lynn Arts, and the Social Documentary Network. While his professional career as a pastor and photographer spans several decades, there is no verified evidence in historical records of his involvement with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization during the late 1960s or early 1970s.

results.possibleIdentity: Joel Heathcote (often referred to as Joel B. Heathcote) is a lighting artist, designer, and multimedia professional whose career spans from the late 1960s to the present. A 1961 graduate of Dartmouth College, he established himself as a specialist in lighting effects and technical documentation. During the late 1960s and early 1970s, Heathcote was active in Laguna Beach, California, where he assisted organizations like the Philosophical Research Society by producing 35mm slides of their art collections and rare books. He is notably recognized as a lighting designer for large-scale multimedia events, including his work on "The World Game" at the University of California, San Diego (UCSD) and San Diego State University (SDSU) in the 1990s—a project deeply influenced by the ideas of Buckminster Fuller. Professionally, he has also specialized in creating fiber optic reproductions of the night sky and high-tech lighting for theatrical productions. His collaborative work at the intersection of art, technology, and engineering, particularly in the San Diego and Laguna Beach areas during the late 1960s and 1970s, matches the profile of participants in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, which promoted similar interdisciplinary collaborations. He has also been involved with the Casque & Gauntlet Senior Society at Dartmouth, serving as its "Merlin of Merlins" and overseeing the society's creative and technical documentation for decades.

results.possibleIdentity: Arthur "Bill" Brody (born March 2, 1943) is an American visual artist and educator who has been active since the mid-1960s. He is best known for his work in painting, printmaking, and early computer art, as well as his pioneering research in virtual reality. Brody earned a BS in Mathematics from Harvey Mudd College in 1965 and an MFA in Painting and Printmaking from Claremont Graduate School in 1967. He moved to Fairbanks, Alaska, in 1967 to teach at the University of Alaska Fairbanks (UAF), where he served as a professor of art until 2000 and was the department chair for nine years. During his tenure at UAF, he developed the university's computer art and printmaking curricula. In the 1970s, he was involved with experimental media, as evidenced by his inclusion in the Phil Morton Memorial Research Archive, which documents the emergence of experimental video and media art education. His later career included research at UAF's Arctic Region Supercomputing Center, where he was the lead researcher for a virtual reality application called BLUi (Body Language User Interface). His artistic practice often involves painting large-scale landscapes en plein air in remote wilderness areas, such as the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge and the Everglades, as well as creating woodcuts and carved metal panels. His work is held in the collections of the Anchorage Museum, the Brooklyn Museum, and the Portland Art Museum.

results.possibleIdentity: B. Reuvers was a researcher and technical professional active in the late 1960s and 1970s, primarily known for work in the field of coatings rheology and paint technology. Reuvers is closely linked to Sven-Erik Orchard, a notable engineer who won a prize in the 1968 Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) competition for the exhibition 'The Machine as Seen at the End of the Mechanical Age' (specifically for the collaborative work 'Linear Approximation' with artist John Pearson). Reuvers co-authored technical papers with Orchard and others (such as N. P. D. Smith) during this period, focusing on the physics of brush marks and the rheological limits of waterborne paints. Given the E.A.T. mission of matching artists with engineers, it is highly likely that B. Reuvers provided technical support or research that facilitated artistic experimentation with coatings, materials, or fluid dynamics.

Bert Reuvers (1931–2017) was a Dutch artist based in Wijchen, Netherlands, who was active from the late 1960s through the early 2010s. He worked in various media, including painting, ceramics, and tile work. His artistic style ranged from realistic miniatures of local landmarks to abstract compositions. He was a board member of the Stichting Museum Kasteel Wijchen and organized several local exhibitions. While his direct involvement with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is not explicitly documented in exhibition catalogs, his active career as an artist in the Netherlands during the height of the international E.A.T. movement and his technical experimentation with ceramic glazes and pigments (such as using red enamel that changes color upon firing) align with the profile of an artist interested in the intersection of traditional craft and chemical processes.

results.possibleIdentity: Fred Weiss (also known as Frederick Weiss) was a technical collaborator and engineer associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). He is historically significant for his role as one of the contributing engineers for the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan. This project, which remains one of E.A.T.'s most famous large-scale collaborations, involved a multidisciplinary team of over 70 artists and engineers. Weiss specifically worked on the technical systems for the pavilion, which featured a sophisticated real-time sound system, a spherical mirror, and fog sculptures. In the context of E.A.T., technical collaborators like Weiss were often recognized as co-creators alongside artists due to the organization's mission of fostering deep cooperation between the two fields.

Fred Weiss was an artist and filmmaker active in the San Francisco and Berkeley experimental art scene during the early 1970s. He was associated with the Cat's Paw Palace of Performing Arts, an alternative artist-run venue in West Berkeley that opened in 1973. He is listed in historical records as a filmmaker within a community of West Coast artists exploring electronic film, video, and laser art. This underground network often shared personnel and aesthetic goals with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, which had established chapters in San Francisco and Los Angeles during that period. Other artists in his circle included pioneers of video art like Stephen Beck and Sharon Grace.

Fred Weiss was a musician and bassist active in the late 1960s. He was a member of the Bluegrass Dropouts, a group that included notable figures like David Grisman, Peter Rowan, and Richard Greene. The band was active around 1968, particularly in the folk and bluegrass scenes of New York and the West Coast. While primarily known for music, the experimental nature of the New York and Berkeley scenes in which he operated often overlapped with the technological and multimedia experiments championed by organizations like E.A.T.

results.possibleIdentity: Daina Krumins (often spelled Krumins, born 1947) is a Latvian-American experimental filmmaker, animator, and visual artist who became active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. Born in a refugee camp in Munich, Germany, she immigrated to the U.S. and studied at the NYU Film School before receiving her MFA from the California Institute of the Arts (CalArts) in 1972, where she specialized in optical printing and special effects. Her work is frequently described as "homegrown Surrealism" and is noted for its meticulous focus on textures and the use of labor-intensive techniques. Her filmography includes 'The Divine Miracle' (1972), which won numerous awards, and 'Babobilicons' (1982), a surreal stop-motion project that took nine years to complete and famously utilized cultivated slime molds and live ladybugs. Professionally, she worked as a rotoscoper and optical printer operator at Lookout Mountain Films for Pat O'Neill and contributed special effects to 'The Empire Strikes Back' (1980), specifically painting auras. She identifies as neurodivergent (Asperger's syndrome), which she cites as a primary influence on her non-linear and texture-based artistic vision. Her connection to Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is consistent with her technical experimentation and her involvement in early multimedia spaces like The Kitchen in New York, where her work was featured as early as 1971.

results.possibleIdentity: Amy Stromsten is an American photographer, writer, and educator born on December 5, 1942, in Detroit, Michigan. She has been an influential figure in the art scene at Rutgers University, specifically Douglass College and the Mason Gross School of the Arts, where she has taught since 1977. Stromsten became active in the late 1960s, receiving a certificate from the Cooper Union School of Art in 1969 and working on art and public relations projects for the New York City Department of Parks in 1967. Throughout the 1970s, she held various teaching positions at the School of Visual Arts, the New School for Social Research, and Parsons School of Design. Her artistic practice is noted for documentary photography and the use of non-silver processes such as cyanotype and Vandyke brown. She was included in the notable Women Artists Series at Douglass College in 1972 and was part of the avant-garde circle at Rutgers that intersected with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement and its co-founder Billy Klüver. Her other professional contributions include producing a documentary on Diane Arbus for WBAI radio in 1973 and directing the Art Center Program at the New Jersey Institute of Technology in 1975.

Stephen Kaltenbach (born 1940) is a prominent American conceptual artist, sculptor, and painter who was a key figure in the New York avant-garde during the late 1960s. After studying at the University of California, Davis, he moved to New York City in 1967, where he became known for experimental and process-oriented works. His practice during this period included his 'Time Capsules' (sealed metal containers with cryptic instructions), bronze 'Sidewalk Plaques' embedded in city pavements, and a series of twelve anonymous advertisements published in Artforum magazine between 1968 and 1969 with 'micro-manifestos' such as 'Become a Legend' and 'Tell a Lie.' Kaltenbach was part of the dynamic conceptual milieu that overlapped with organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.); he participated in seminal exhibitions of that era, including '9 at Leo Castelli' (1968), Harald Szeemann’s 'When Attitudes Become Form' (1969), and the 'Information' exhibition at the Museum of Modern Art (1970). In 1970, at the height of his recognition, he abruptly left the New York art scene and returned to California to teach at California State University, Sacramento, a move he later described as a conceptual act of 'relinquishment' to subvert his own reputation. In California, he continued his work under various personas and produced large-scale paintings such as 'Portrait of My Father' (1972–1979).

results.possibleIdentity: Gerda Tasgal Kliegman (1908–1996) was an artist who lived and worked on the North Fork of Long Island, New York. She is recognized by the Southold Historical Museum as part of a significant group of artists active in the region before 1970. Her life and work are documented in the publication 'A Shared Aesthetic: Artists of Long Island's North Fork' (2008). During the late 1960s and early 1970s, she was part of the local artistic community that intersected with the broader New York art scene, which at the time included organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). She was married to Israel Kliegman, with whom she was also associated in business ventures such as the Gerdan Realty Corp.
